retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: One of the primary mechanisms by which autoimmunity is prevented is through the expression of Aire (autoimmune regulator) genes by medullary epithelial cells. Aire genes are responsible for allowing the production of tissue-specific antigens that get presented to T-lymphocytes. Peptide antigens that result from Aire gene product function represent the self-antigens located throughout the rest of the body. Failure of Aire gene expression leads to autoimmunity. Positive and negative selection are highly specific processes in the development of T lymphocytes, ultimately leading to the death of approximately 95% of cortical T cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: T lymphocytes capable of binding MHC class I or II on cTECs then migrate to the thymic medulla where they undergo negative selection. During negative selection, T lymphocytes come into contact with self-antigen presented by macrophages and cytoreticular TECs. [4] If the T lymphocyte’s TCR binds these self-antigens too strongly, the T lymphocyte will be targeted and undergo apoptosis in order to ensure immune tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: T lymphocytes first undergo positive selection in the cortex, where they get exposed to major histocompatibility complex (MHC) molecules on cortical thymic epithelial cells (cTEC). This process ensures that the T lymphocytes contain TCRs capable of recognizing and binding to antigen displayed by MHC class I or MHC class II molecules located on the surface of TECs within the cortex. T lymphocytes that are unable to recognize self-antigen presented by MHC I or MHC II molecules are targeted for apoptosis and phagocytized by macrophages. [4] T lymphocytes that successfully complete positive selection retain only one co-receptor, either CD4 or CD8 depending on which MHC molecule was recognized by the TCR. These are referred to as single positive (SP) T-cells. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical significance of the blood-thymus barrier is still extensively studied because of its potential role in limiting autoimmune diseases. Mouse studies demonstrate that intrathymic inoculation of antigen has led to apoptosis of CD4 and CD8 cells. [11] Studies have found that genetically engineered autologous dendritic cells expressing self-antigen could be inoculated into the thymic cortex to treat autoimmune disease. Genetically engineered dendritic cells with self-antigen can also be inoculated into the thymic cortex to promote tolerance for organ transplantation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Myeloma antigens in mice have been phagocytized and presented by dendritic cells within the thymic cortex. [12] Presentation of tumor antigen to the cortical thymocytes can lead to apoptosis, constituting a major mechanism of tumor escape. The role of the blood-thymus barrier is still the subject of intensive research because of its potential in treating various autoimmune diseases and cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Autoimmunity -- Mechanism -- T-cell Central Tolerance
retrieved_docs.content: Central tolerance is the initial way autoreactive T-cells are prevented from entering the systemic circulation. This is an effective process largely due to the medullary epithelial cells in the thymus. To present a comprehensive array of self-peptides found in all body organs, these cells express autoimmune regulator transcription factors (AIRE) that cause increased expression of tissue-restricted antigens found in other parts of the body. Expression of tissue-restricted antigens aids with efficient negative selection. Autoimmunity can develop in cases where mutations arise in AIRE, causing less expression of tissue-restricted antigens. An example of this is the disease autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type I (APECED), characterized by at least 2 of the following 3 conditions: candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and Addison disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: T lymphocytes arise from stem cells in the bone marrow before traveling to the thymus to mature into T cells. The thymic cortex contains immature, undifferentiated T cells that will first build a T cell receptor via V(D)J recombination. Successful completion of a T cell receptor (TCR) that can signal from the cell surface via the CD3 signaling complex results in upregulation of both the CD4 and CD8 coreceptor molecules. These cells are referred to as double-positive (DP) thymocytes due to the presence of both CD4 and CD8.

retrieved_docs.title: Thymectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Physiologically, the thymus is a critical organ for cellular immunity. Immature lymphocytes from the bone marrow migrate to the thymus, where they undergo maturation and selection to become functional T-cells. Positive selection occurs in the cortex, ensuring thymocytes can recognize self-major histocompatibility complex molecules, while negative selection in the medulla eliminates autoreactive cells to prevent autoimmunity. Through this process, the thymus helps establish self-tolerance and produces cytotoxic and helper T-cells capable of initiating immune responses against pathogens. MG is the most common autoimmune disorder associated with the thymus, caused by autoantibodies to acetylcholine receptors, resulting in muscle fatigue. Thymomas, the most common anterior mediastinal tumors, are often linked to MG, which is characterized by autoantibodies to acetylcholine receptors, resulting in muscle fatigue (with continued contractions), and can also affect ocular, facial, oropharyngeal, and limb muscles. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Thyroid Gland -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochemical techniques are essential for diagnosing thyroid lesions of the thyroid gland pathologies (see Image 2. Hashimoto Thyroiditis). Direct and indirect immunofluorescence techniques are helpful to localize the thyroid hormones tri-iodothyronine (T3) and thyroxine (T4). The techniques manipulate the specificity of antibodies for T3 or T4, which gives enhanced fluorescence for the apical cytoplasm of follicular cells and the colloid pools, indicating thyroglobulin. The technique is specific for the thyroid gland and does not stain the adjacent gland. [19] Histochemical analyses of the patterns of activity and localization of 5-nucleotidase (CD73) through the use of anti-5' nucleotidase ecto antibody in the thyroid gland provide valuable insight into several pathologies associated with the thyroid gland. The activity of the enzyme is increased in papillary thyroid cancer (PTC). Monitoring the expression patterns is used to observe tumor size and lymph node metastasis in PTC. Additionally, the sites of localization of this enzyme also indicate cell transport mechanisms. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Thymoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Immunohistochemistry is helpful in identifying thymomas. Thymic epithelial cells are positive for keratins, epithelial membrane antigens, p63, p40, and PAX8. Thymic lymphocytes stain with terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT), CD1a, CD3, CD45, and CD99. Subsets of thymoma subtype A and AB stain for CD20 and rarely thyroid transcription factor (TTF-1). Thymomas have an unusually low mutation burden. The most common mutations in thymic tumors include: General transcription factor I (39% to 42%) p53(25% to 36%) KIT(6-20%) Cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor 2A (11% - also found in thymic carcinoma and B3 thymoma) A rare occurrence - n-ras, k-ras, cyclin D1, anaplastic lymphoma kinase, ataxia-telangiectasia mutated, ERBB4, FGFR3, SMARCB1, and STK11 genes

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thymus -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: T-cell development within the thymus involves both positive and negative selection. During positive selection, T-cells capable of recognizing self-antigens are retained for further screening, while those lacking appropriate receptor affinity undergo apoptosis. Approximately 95% of developing T-cells are eliminated at this stage due to self-reactivity. Surviving cells then undergo negative selection, during which those that bind self-antigens with high affinity are also removed to prevent autoimmunity.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thymus -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Only lymphocytes that pass both selection processes exit the thymus. Once in the periphery, these mature T-cells become activated in response to pathogens such as bacteria and viruses. Clonal expansion follows activation. After pathogen clearance, most effector T-cells undergo apoptosis, while a subset persists as memory cells, facilitating rapid and robust responses upon reexposure to the same antigen.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The cortex contains three different types of TECs, each of which are involved in antigen presentation using MHC class I or II molecules. These epithelial cells are unique in their ability to present antigen using MHC class II since this function is reserved for macrophages, dendritic cells, and B cells throughout the rest of the body. There are cortical TECs (cTEC) that surround the microvasculature, lining the connective tissue of the septa and capsule, and play an important role in antigen presentation during positive selection. There are medullary TECs that play an important role in antigen presentation during negative selection. Lastly, there are stellate TECs, which form a cytoreticulum and also function in antigen presentation to immature thymocytes as well as cytokine secretion.

retrieved_docs.title: Thymic Hyperplasia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The thymus got its role in T-cell differentiation discovered a few decades ago before the 1960s it was considered vestigial. Though largely known as a lymphoid organ and for its role in T-cell differentiation, thymus also has an endocrine role that includes manufacturing thymosin that regulates T-cell differentiation and other humoral factors that regulate cell growth, maturation and guide the process of mineralization. [1] The thymus is the principal location for T-cell lymphopoiesis. The production of functional T-cell is choreographed by thymic epithelial cells that guide them in proliferation, maturation, and survival. The thymic epithelial cells are compartmentalized into two main populations medullary thymic epithelial cells and cortical thymic epithelial cells. These populations are further segregated into distinct subpopulations having specific molecular and functional roles. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, T-Cell Lymphocyte -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: All T lymphocytes have the TCR (T-cell receptor) and the pan-T-cell co-receptor CD3. It recognizes the antigen-loaded MHC molecules on other cells. Whether it can recognize MHC 1 or MHC 2 depends on its co-receptor (rather CD4 or CD8) as previously discussed. The TCR itself is a transmembrane receptor made up of various alpha and beta chains, each with variable regions. These variations develop to recognize different antigens and become selected for during the T-cell maturation process (involving positive and negative selection, within the thymus). [1] [2] A small portion of T cells in the body have delta and gamma chains and recognize different antigen types than other T cells. These cells are a unique subpopulation called gamma-delta T cells and reside mostly in the mucosal epithelial layers of the body. [3] T cells also have various chemokine/co-receptors on their surfaces. The CCR5 and CXCR4 (found mostly on CD4+ T cells) chemokine receptors are of particular importance because they are the targets of HIV. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The main purpose of the blood thymus barrier is to prevent cortical T lymphocytes from interacting with foreign macromolecules. Without the presence of this barrier, foreign antigens could cross the cortical capillaries and pose as self-antigens within the cortex. Mouse model studies have shown that intrathymic inoculation of foreign antigens leads to immune tolerance since thymocytes cannot distinguish self-antigen from the foreign antigen. Allowing foreign antigens to enter the thymus and influence thymocyte development can allow a developing T cell to become tolerant to the antigen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled growth of cancerous cells. The thyroid‑originating tumor that spreads to cervical lymph nodes illustrates how a primary malignant neoplasm can metastasize, while the primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus exemplifies a malignant proliferation arising directly within the thymic tissue. Both conditions underscore the importance of recognizing malignant neoplastic proliferations as pathological processes that require accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted oncologic management.

Renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) are specific entities within cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis; partial or complete nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية) is the surgical intervention commonly associated with these renal tumors. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and malignant or uncertain tumor cells define primary and secondary (metastatic) disease across multiple organ systems—lymph nodes, bone, skin, respiratory and intrathoracic organs, endocrine glands, thymus, breast, ovary, kidney, and pancreas—providing clinicians with a structured framework for cancer origin identification, staging, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 254
retrieved_docs.content: as a pivotal concept that connects a variety of primary tumors (breast, thyroid, skin, bone, thymus, endocrine glands, respiratory and intrathoracic organs) with their secondary sites (bone, ovary, pancreas), and situates specific neoplastic entities within broader classifications of malignant and uncertain‑behavior neoplasms, thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding metastatic disease pathways and their relevance to patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1139
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic cancer (referred to as “kanker; metastase”) is classified as a specific form of malignant neoplasm that lacks a defined primary site, making it a child of the broader category “malignant neoplasm without specification of site.” This metastatic entity can arise in several organ systems, including the skin (سرطان الجلد النقيلي), the respiratory and intrathoracic organs, and endocrine glands such as the thymus. The metastatic disease of the thymus is a more specific subclass of metastatic malignant neoplasms of endocrine glands, indicating that thymic involvement represents a particular manifestation of endocrine‑gland metastasis. Clinically, metastatic lesions are recognized as a key finding that signals the presence of both thymic metastatic disease and unspecified malignant neoplasms. The metastatic process is closely linked to thyroid cancer (سرطان الغدة الدرقية), which itself is a type of malignant tumor (ورم صماعي خبيث). Across these entities, the term “النقيلة” (denoting malignancy) is repeatedly associated, underscoring the malignant nature of the primary and metastatic neoplasms. Together, these relationships highlight that metastatic cancers can present in diverse anatomical locations, are identified through characteristic lesion findings, and are intrinsically connected to other malignant tumors such as thyroid carcinoma, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of metastatic disease in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic cancer (“kanker; metastase”) is positioned as a central node that connects organ‑specific metastatic entities and the broader category “malignant neoplasm without specification of site.” It has the child lesion “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (metastatic skin cancer) and is linked upward to the generic unspecified malignant neoplasm. Clinically, this metastatic cancer is associated with thyroid carcinoma (“سرطان الغدة الدرقية”) and with ovarian metastasis (“نقائل إلى المبيض”), illustrating spread to both endocrine and gynecologic sites. Metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organs includes a subordinate “kanker; metastase” linked hierarchically to a metastatic malignant neoplasm of the thymus, and a metastatic malignant neoplasm of an endocrine gland also lists “kanker; metastase” as a child, reinforcing that endocrine tissues can serve as sources or targets of metastatic spread. Histopathologically, a “Malignant Cellular Infiltrate” is recorded as a disease‑finding for cancer

Thyroid cancer (سرطان الغدة الدرقية) is classified as a malignant neoplasm (ورم صماوي خبيث), placing it within the overarching cancerous tumor category. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.
